title: The Long War Sets In: Blockades, Collapsing Currencies, and the Fracturing of Order
subhead: As the Iran war enters its third month with no end in sight, the geopolitical shockwaves are spreading far beyond the Strait of Hormuz — from OPEC's fragmentation to central banks scrambling on inflation, from Congress grilling the Pentagon to Gaza's expanding occupation zones.

sections.heading: The War Drags On: Trump Digs In for a Long Blockade
sections.body:
Day 60 of the US-Israel war on Iran, and President Trump is signaling anything but a quick exit. With talks stalled despite Pakistani mediation efforts, Trump publicly warned Iran to 'get smart soon' and accept a nuclear deal — while privately discussing extending the naval blockade of Iranian ports for months. US intelligence agencies are reportedly gaming out scenarios for how Tehran would respond to a unilateral American victory declaration, an indication that Washington is searching for an off-ramp that doesn't look like retreat.

Pentagon chief Pete Hegseth faced his first congressional grilling since the war began, testifying that the operation has cost $25 billion so far and insisting the US must push Iran 'to the point of giving up' its nuclear ambitions. But with Iranian officials demanding 'credible guarantees' against future attacks and Trump refusing to lift pressure, the diplomatic track remains frozen. The blockade, the sanctions, the military campaign — all point to a strategy of attrition, not negotiation.
sections.whyItMatters: A protracted blockade strategy transforms a 'limited' conflict into an open-ended economic war with global ramifications. Every day the Strait remains partially closed, oil markets recalibrate, inflation spreads, and political costs mount for all sides. Trump appears willing to absorb domestic criticism — his approval has sunk to 34% — betting that Iran's economy will break before American patience does.

sections.heading: Iran's Economy Crumbles: The Rial Hits 1.8 Million to the Dollar
sections.body:
Iran's currency collapsed to a historic low on Wednesday — 1.8 million rials to the dollar, down from relative stability just weeks ago. The plunge reflects the cumulative impact of tightened US sanctions, the naval blockade choking off oil exports, and war-induced capital flight. Tehran residents report soaring prices for basic goods, with import-dependent supply chains severed by the American interdiction campaign.

The economic warfare component is working as intended: squeeze the Iranian state's revenue streams, force domestic hardship, and create political pressure for concessions. But the strategy carries risks. A collapsing economy doesn't necessarily produce a compliant government — it can radicalize populations, strengthen hardliners, and eliminate the very moderates who might negotiate. History is littered with regimes that survived sanctions through repression rather than reform.
sections.whyItMatters: Currency collapse is the canary in the coal mine for state stability. When a government loses control of its money, it loses control of everything else — salaries, subsidies, security forces. The question isn't whether Iran hurts; it's whether that pain produces capitulation or collapse. Neither outcome guarantees regional stability.

sections.heading: OPEC Fractures: UAE Walks Away
sections.body:
The United Arab Emirates has formally announced its departure from OPEC, effective May 1. The decision strips the cartel of one of its most important producers and opens the door to an all-out price war once the Iran conflict settles. Reuters analysts note that the 65-year-old organization's influence over global oil markets will sharply diminish, with Gulf producers now free to compete for market share without production quotas.

The UAE's exit is the second major shock to OPEC in recent weeks and reflects the broader realignment of Middle Eastern powers. Abu Dhabi, feeling excluded from US-Iran ceasefire discussions despite its proximity to the conflict, is hedging its bets. The message to Riyadh is clear: security guarantees from Washington can't be counted on, so energy leverage must be secured independently.
sections.whyItMatters: A weakened OPEC means a more volatile oil market. With the UAE free to pump at will and Saudi Arabia likely to follow suit to maintain market share, the post-war energy landscape could see a race to the bottom on prices — or a scramble for control if Hormuz remains constrained. Either way, the era of coordinated production management is ending.

sections.heading: Inflation Goes Global: Central Banks in Crisis Mode
sections.body:
From Ottawa to Bangkok to Sydney, central banks are holding emergency meetings and revising forecasts upward as the oil shock ripples through the global economy. The Bank of Canada held rates steady at 2.25% but warned inflation will peak this month before — hopefully — moderating. Thailand's central bank cited 'the impact of higher oil prices driven by war in the Middle East' while cutting growth forecasts. Germany reported inflation accelerating to 2.9% in April, driven entirely by surging energy costs. Australia's consumer prices surged in Q1, with core inflation 'uncomfortably high' and a rate hike looming.

The common thread: policymakers who thought they'd tamed inflation after the pandemic shock are now watching it reignite from an entirely different source. The oil-price-transmission mechanism is familiar — higher fuel costs become higher freight costs become higher food costs become embedded inflation expectations. But the policy toolkit is limited. Central banks can't raise rates enough to offset a supply shock without crushing growth, and fiscal responses risk making inflation worse.
sections.whyItMatters: This is the Iran war's most underappreciated consequence: it's not just a regional conflict, it's a global economic shock. Every percentage point of additional inflation erodes real wages, increases debt burdens, and fuels political instability in countries far from the Strait of Hormuz. The war's true costs are being exported worldwide.

sections.heading: Gaza's Shadow War: Israel Expands Military Control
sections.body:
While the world focuses on Iran, Israel is quietly redrawing the map in Gaza. New maps issued by Israeli authorities just over a month ago designate an expanded 'zone of military control' that puts thousands of displaced Palestinians in fear of being deemed military targets. The zone represents a significant expansion of Israeli authority within Gaza's interior, beyond the border-adjacent areas previously controlled.

Meanwhile, Israeli strikes continue in southern Lebanon, with Reuters reporting that five people were killed Tuesday, including three rescuers who arrived to help wounded from an initial attack. Lebanese officials report that Israeli officers describe their mission as focused on 'systematic destruction' of buildings rather than combat operations — a shift from targeting Hezbollah infrastructure to a broader campaign of demolition.
sections.whyItMatters: The Gaza and Lebanon operations reveal how regional powers are using the cover of the Iran war to pursue long-held objectives. For Israel, the moment presents an opportunity: global attention is fixed on Tehran, US political cover is assured, and the costs of expansion go largely unnoticed. The long-term strategic calculation is clear — create facts on the ground while the world looks elsewhere.

sections.heading: King Charles in Washington: Soft Power Meets Hard Reality
sections.body:
Britain's King Charles III delivered a rare address to Congress, emphasizing transatlantic unity and — in a pointed moment — stating that he does not want Iran to acquire nuclear weapons. The speech was carefully crafted to be apolitical, but the context was impossible to ignore: a British monarch endorsing, however subtly, the American position on the war while Trump stood beside him. The King will visit New York's 9/11 memorial today, reinforcing the historical bonds the Anglo-American alliance is meant to represent.

Trump, characteristically, revealed he was 'jealous' of the King following the address — a strange moment of vulnerability from a president who typically projects dominance. The state visit, with its ceremonies and toasts, papered over the reality that the special relationship is being tested by events neither side fully controls.
sections.whyItMatters: Symbolic diplomacy matters, but symbols can't halt geopolitical currents. The King's visit was meant to project continuity and solidarity at a moment of profound disruption. Whether that projection can survive a prolonged Middle Eastern war, economic dislocation, and transatlantic disagreement over strategy remains an open question.

bottomLine: The Iran war has entered a grim new phase: neither side can claim victory, neither can afford to lose, and both are settling in for a protracted struggle. The economic dimensions — blockades, sanctions, inflation, a fracturing OPEC — have transformed a regional conflict into a global crisis. Central banks from Canada to Australia are scrambling; currencies from Tehran to emerging markets are under pressure; and the price of oil remains a sword of Damocles over the world economy. Meanwhile, regional actors are using the distraction to pursue their own agendas — Israel in Gaza and Lebanon, the UAE in OPEC, Russia in Africa. Sixty days in, the war's true costs are only beginning to be counted.
